articles.title: CSS vs. JavaScript
articles.summary: CSS vs JavaScript Exploring the performance implications of different animation strategies has been a common question around animation performance. The conventional wisdom suggests that JS-based animations are slower than CSS-based ones due to additional work required for calculating x values on every frame and disrupting application threads. However, modern browser engines can handle this without affecting framerate, making CSS transitions and keyframe animations more suitable for most applications.

articles.title: A new register allocator for ZJIT
articles.summary: A new register allocator has been implemented in ZJIT, allowing compilers to keep variables in registers as long as possible by allocating them to specific registers. This approach can significantly improve performance by reducing memory accesses. A linear scan register allocator based on Christian Wimmer's paper is used, which balances compile-time and code quality trade-offs. The new allocator also takes into account the Static single-assignment form representation of code, where variables are assigned once in SSA form.

articles.title: Atom Exhaustion Is Not a Footgun. It's One Third of Our CVEs
articles.summary: Atom exhaustion is a denial-of-service vulnerability that occurs when atoms are not garbage collected in the BEAM ecosystem. This can happen when creating atoms from non-finite values, especially user-supplied input, or when using certain interpolation patterns. The issue persists because it often appears in code where input was assumed to be controlled or finite, and is exacerbated by the lack of a finite set of possible values for some schemes. Linters can help catch these patterns before they become vulnerabilities, making explicit lookup tables or safer existing-atom variants more practical approaches.

articles.title: A brief note about slot access cost in Common Lisp
articles.summary: A brief note about slot access cost in Common Lisp, which is a key technology for developers to understand the differences between structure objects and standard objects when it comes to accessing their slots. Structure objects have undefined behavior due to modifying the structure class, while instances track whether they are up-to-date or obsolete, allowing for flexibility in memory layout changes. This requires developers to verify if an instance is obsolete before calling generic functions that rely on inlined readers.

articles.title: Go: Support for Generic Methods
articles.summary: Go has introduced support for generic methods, which allow developers to create functions that can work with any type, without having to declare them as interface methods. By allowing methods to have a receiver of a generic type, Go provides a way to implement an interface while still maintaining the benefits of methods for organizing code and accessing types through their namespace.
